events.text: The patient is a 62-year-old female named Margaret Tran. She is of Vietnamese-American ethnicity, born in Saigon and immigrated to the United States at age 14. She is a former smoker with a 12 pack-year history, having quit 18 years prior to diagnosis. She works as a high school biology teacher. She has a history of well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism. Her ECOG performance status at initial presentation is 1.

At age 62, the patient underwent a CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained after presenting to her primary care physician with a 6-week history of progressive exertional dyspnea, mild right-sided chest discomfort, and a 4 kg weight loss. CT chest demonstrated a 4.8 cm spiculated right lower lobe mass with ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy (stations 4R, 7). No pleural effusion. CT abdomen/pelvis showed no hepatic, adrenal, or intraabdominal metastases. Impression: Right lower lobe mass with mediastinal adenopathy, highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy. Recommended PET/CT and tissue biopsy.

At age 62, the patient underwent a PET/CT scan. FDG-avid 4.8 cm right lower lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2). Hypermetabolic right paratracheal (4R) and subcarinal (7) lymph nodes with SUVmax 9.8 and 11.3, respectively. No FDG-avid distant metastases. No brain imaging included. Impression: Stage IIIB by metabolic imaging criteria. Brain MRI recommended for complete staging.

At age 62, the patient underwent MRI brain with and without contrast for staging evaluation. No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal brain parenchyma. Impression: No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease at this time.

At age 62, the patient underwent CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of the right lower lobe mass. Pathology report: Adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated. Histologic features include acinar and lepidic growth patterns. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive, Napsin A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative. PD-L1 TPS by 22C3 assay: 15%. Specimen adequacy confirmed as sufficient for molecular testing. Diagnosis: Lung adenocarcinoma.

At age 62 years, the patient received a diagnosis of stage IIIB (T3N2M0, AJCC 8th edition) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE LUNG (ICD-O histology 8140/3), primary site: RIGHT LOWER LOBE (ICD-O site C34.31). Summary stage: Regional by lymph node involvement. Relevant biomarkers: PD-L1 TPS 15% (22C3 assay). EGFR L858R mutation confirmed by tissue NGS (see separate report). ALK, ROS1 rearrangements: negative. KRAS: wild type. MET: no amplification. Tumor size: 4.8 cm. Lymph node involvement: ipsilateral mediastinal (4R, 7). Confirmation: Positive histology via CT-guided biopsy. Laterality: Right. Grade: Moderately differentiated (G2). Histologic subtype: Acinar and lepidic predominant adenocarcinoma.

At age 62 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for lung adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right lower lobe primary tumor (CT-guided biopsy). Comprehensive solid tumor panel (523-gene panel, tumor-normal paired analysis): Actionable/clinically significant alterations: EGFR p.L858R (exon 21 missense mutation, allele frequency 34.2%, pathogenic, tier 1 actionable); TP53 p.R175H (exon 5 missense, allele frequency 41.8%, pathogenic co-mutation, tier 2); STK11 wild type; KEAP1 wild type; MET exon 14 skipping: absent; MET copy number: 2.1 (no amplification); ERBB2 (HER2): wild type, no amplification; RET fusion: negative; ROS1 fusion: negative; ALK fusion: negative; MYC copy number 2.4 (no amplification); CDKN2A: heterozygous deletion; RB1: wild type; PIK3CA: wild type; SMAD4: wild type; NF1: wild type. Microsatellite instability status: MSS. Tumor mutational burden: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low). KRAS: wild type (confirmed). BRAF: wild type. Germline analysis: No pathogenic germline variants identified in cancer predisposition genes. Overall interpretation: EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation with co-mutant TP53 p.R175H. Recommendation: EGFR-directed targeted therapy (osimertinib preferred based on FLAURA data).

At age 62, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board discussion and initial oncology consultation. The case was reviewed by thoracic surgery, radiation oncology, and medical oncology. Given the stage IIIB (T3N2M0) disease, the patient is not a surgical candidate. The multidisciplinary team discussed chemoradiation versus targeted therapy given the EGFR L858R mutation. The LAURA trial data supporting osimertinib maintenance after concurrent chemoradiation was reviewed; however, given the patient's performance status, co-morbidities, and preference to avoid upfront chemoradiation toxicity, the decision was made to initiate osimertinib as first-line targeted therapy. The patient was counseled extensively regarding treatment options, expected response rates, and the anticipated development of resistance. She is agreeable to proceed with osimertinib 80 mg daily. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present. No prior systemic treatment.

At age 62 years, the patient initiated curative/disease-control-intent osimertinib (TAGRISSO) 80 mg orally daily as first-line therapy for stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma. Treatment rationale: third-generation EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor with activity against L858R and T790M mutations, with superior CNS penetration. Baseline labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs within normal limits. EKG: QTc 432 ms (normal). Thyroid function: TSH 2.1 (stable on levothyroxine). Concurrent medications: levothyroxine 88 mcg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily.

At age 62 (approximately 6 weeks after starting osimertinib), the patient developed grade 2 acneiform/papulopustular rash involving the face, neck, and upper chest. She also reported grade 1 paronychia of the right index and middle fingers. Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools per day above baseline) also noted. Management: Topical clindamycin 1% gel and oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily initiated for the rash. Osimertinib dose maintained at 80 mg daily. Paronychia managed with soaks and topical antibiotics. Patient counseled on skin care regimen. Diarrhea managed with loperamide as needed.

At age 62 (3 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast. Right lower lobe mass decreased from 4.8 cm to 2.9 cm (39.6% reduction by RECIST 1.1). Mediastinal lymphadenopathy (4R and subcarinal) markedly decreased in size and now sub-centimeter. No new lesions. No pleural effusion. No hepatic or adrenal involvement. Impression: Partial response to osimertinib by RECIST 1.1 criteria. Continued treatment recommended.

At age 62 (3-month oncology follow-up), the patient reports improved dyspnea and resolution of chest discomfort. Weight stable. ECOG PS 1 (improved from initial presentation). The rash has responded well to doxycycline and topical treatment, now grade 1. Paronychia resolved. Diarrhea manageable with loperamide. CT scan demonstrates partial response. Plan: Continue osimertinib 80 mg daily. Discontinue doxycycline given improved rash. Continue topical clindamycin. Repeat imaging at 6 months. Cancer present with response to therapy.

At age 62-63 (9 months after initiating osimertinib, approximately 9 months on treatment), the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Right lower lobe mass further decreased to 1.8 cm. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy no longer visualized on CT. No new pulmonary nodules. No hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastases identified. Impression: Continued partial response, ongoing improvement. Continued osimertinib treatment supported.

At age 63 (9-month follow-up), the patient is doing well clinically. ECOG PS 0-1. Dyspnea resolved. Weight has returned to baseline. Mild grade 1 xerosis and residual paronychia managed conservatively. Hypothyroidism remains well-controlled. Labs show CBC, CMP, LFTs within normal limits. TSH 3.4. CT demonstrates continued excellent response. Patient expresses significant relief and has returned to full-time teaching. Plan: Continue osimertinib 80 mg daily. Next imaging at month 12. Cancer present but responding to therapy.

At age 63 (14 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. New finding: Right lower lobe mass has increased from 1.8 cm to 3.1 cm (compared to prior measurement 5 months ago). Additionally, a new 1.4 cm right adrenal nodule is identified that is new compared to prior imaging. Multiple new small bilateral pulmonary nodules (largest 8 mm in right upper lobe). No pleural effusion. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy recurrent at station 4R (1.2 cm). Impression: Radiographic progression by RECIST 1.1 criteria after 14 months of osimertinib. New adrenal metastasis suspected. Urgent brain MRI and biopsy of a progressing lesion recommended.

At age 63 (14 months on treatment), the patient underwent urgent MRI brain with and without gadolinium contrast following CT evidence of systemic progression. New findings: Three intracranial lesions identified: (1) right cerebellar hemisphere 1.1 cm enhancing lesion with surrounding edema, (2) left frontal lobe 0.8 cm enhancing lesion, (3) right parietal 0.6 cm enhancing lesion. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No mass effect sufficient to warrant urgent neurosurgical intervention. Impression: New brain metastases (3 lesions), consistent with progression on osimertinib. Neurosurgical and radiation oncology consultation recommended urgently.

At age 63, the patient underwent CT-guided biopsy of the right adrenal nodule to assess for metastatic disease and to obtain tissue for resistance mechanism evaluation. Pathology: Metastatic adenocarcinoma consistent with lung primary. TTF-1 positive. Molecular/resistance testing sent (see NGS report). Impression: Metastatic lung adenocarcinoma to right adrenal gland, confirming systemic progression.

At age 63 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for lung adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the right adrenal metastasis (CT-guided biopsy) at time of osimertinib progression. Liquid biopsy (plasma ctDNA) also performed concurrently. Tissue panel (523-gene panel): EGFR p.L858R retained (allele frequency 28.1%); EGFR p.T790M acquired resistance mutation (allele frequency 19.4%, pathogenic, tier 1 - acquired osimertinib resistance mechanism); EGFR C797S: NOT detected; TP53 p.R175H retained (allele frequency 35.2%); MET amplification: copy number 3.8 (borderline, not meeting threshold for high-level amplification); MET exon 14 skipping: absent; ERBB2 amplification: copy number 3.1 (low-level gain, not actionable threshold); PIK3CA p.E545K: newly detected (allele frequency 8.3%, subclonal, tier 2); CDK4 amplification: copy number 6.2 (gained); CCND1: wild type; RB1: wild type; FGFR1: wild type. Plasma ctDNA concordant: EGFR L858R and T790M detected, MET borderline gain detected. Overall interpretation: EGFR T790M acquired resistance mutation is the dominant osimertinib resistance mechanism. Co-occurring subclonal PIK3CA E545K and low-level CDK4 amplification may represent additional resistance contributors. No actionable bypass track resistance (no high-level MET amp, no ERBB2 amp, no RET/ALK/ROS1 fusion). Tumor clonal evolution consistent with selective pressure from third-generation EGFR TKI.

At age 63, the patient had an urgent multidisciplinary oncology reassessment following confirmed progression on osimertinib with new brain metastases (3 lesions), systemic progression (lung primary enlarging, new adrenal metastasis, new bilateral pulmonary nodules), and acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation. The patient is neurologically intact with no focal deficits, headache, or seizures; ECOG PS 1. Multidisciplinary tumor board discussed management. Plan: (1) Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to the 3 brain metastases given oligometastatic CNS disease, favorable performance status, and no indication for whole-brain radiation at this time; (2) Discontinue osimertinib after SRS delivery; (3) Initiate platinum-doublet chemotherapy (carboplatin/pemetrexed) given systemic progression. The T790M mutation, while typically targeted by osimertinib, indicates acquired resistance to the current osimertinib regimen; systemic chemotherapy is appropriate as next-line given osimertinib failure. Clinical trial options discussed but no suitable trial immediately available at this institution. Cancer present, progressing on current therapy.

At age 63, the patient underwent Gamma Knife stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to three intracranial metastases. Treatment plan: (1) Right cerebellar lesion 1.1 cm: 20 Gy in 1 fraction; (2) Left frontal lesion 0.8 cm: 22 Gy in 1 fraction; (3) Right parietal lesion 0.6 cm: 24 Gy in 1 fraction. Isodose line 50%. Total treatment time approximately 85 minutes. Treatment well-tolerated. Post-treatment observation: 1 hour without acute neurological changes. Patient discharged home the same day with dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for 5 days then taper. Treatment intent: palliative/local control.

At age 63, approximately 2 weeks following stereotactic radiosurgery, the patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 headache, likely related to post-SRS inflammatory reaction and dexamethasone taper. No focal neurological deficits. No seizures. Managed with continuation of dexamethasone taper extended over 10 days total, then discontinued. Headache resolved with acetaminophen. No dose modification of subsequently initiated chemotherapy required. Grade 1 nausea also noted at initiation of carboplatin/pemetrexed.

At age 63 years, the patient initiated palliative-intent carboplatin AUC 5 intravenously on day 1 plus pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 intravenously on day 1 every 21 days as second-line systemic therapy for stage IV (M1c) EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma following progression on osimertinib. Osimertinib was discontinued. Pre-medications: dexamethasone 4 mg BID day prior, day of, and day after pemetrexed; folic acid 400 mcg daily initiated 7 days prior and continued throughout; vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks. Cycle 1 administered. Baseline: ECOG PS 1, CBC and CMP within normal limits, creatinine clearance 68 mL/min (adequate for pemetrexed). Body surface area: 1.67 m2.

At age 63 (after cycle 2 of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient developed grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 42,000/uL on day 14 of cycle 2). Grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL). Grade 1 neutropenia. No febrile neutropenia. No bleeding events. Management: Carboplatin dose reduced to AUC 4 for subsequent cycles. Pemetrexed maintained at full dose. Growth factor support (filgrastim) not initiated given no febrile neutropenia and neutropenia grade 1. Patient required platelet transfusion (1 unit single donor) for symptomatic thrombocytopenia with epistaxis. Cycle 3 delayed by 1 week to allow hematologic recovery.

At age 63 (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed, approximately 10 weeks after SRS), the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain. CT findings: Right lower lobe mass decreased from 3.1 cm to 2.4 cm. Right adrenal metastasis stable at 1.4 cm. Bilateral pulmonary nodules stable. No new sites of disease. MRI brain: Three known lesion sites show treatment response - right cerebellar lesion decreased to 0.6 cm with central necrosis, left frontal lesion decreased to 0.4 cm, right parietal lesion decreased to 0.3 cm. No new intracranial lesions. No leptomeningeal disease. Impression: Partial response in chest (primary and pleural), stable adrenal disease. Excellent intracranial response to SRS. Cancer present, partial response to platinum doublet chemotherapy.

At age 63, the patient completed 4 cycles of carboplatin (AUC 4 after dose reduction)/pemetrexed and is being assessed for maintenance therapy options and clinical trial eligibility. ECOG PS 1. Patient reports significant fatigue (grade 1-2), persistent grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron, and grade 1 peripheral neuropathy (fingertip paresthesias) attributed to carboplatin. She has noted improved dyspnea compared to prior to chemotherapy. CT scan after 3 cycles demonstrated partial response. The patient and her family have expressed strong interest in clinical trial participation. Referral made to the thoracic oncology clinical trials program. Pemetrexed maintenance (500 mg/m2 every 21 days) initiated pending trial enrollment. Cancer present, responding to carboplatin/pemetrexed.

At age 63 years, the patient initiated pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 intravenously every 21 days as maintenance therapy following 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed for stage IV EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma (post-osimertinib progression). Pre-medications and supplementation (folic acid, vitamin B12) continued. Maintenance pemetrexed administered as a bridge while clinical trial screening and enrollment is completed.

At age 63-64 (following 2 cycles of pemetrexed maintenance and during clinical trial screening), the patient underwent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. Right lower lobe mass stable at 2.3 cm (compared to 2.4 cm on prior imaging). Right adrenal metastasis decreased slightly to 1.1 cm. No new lesions. Bilateral pulmonary nodules stable. Impression: Stable disease on pemetrexed maintenance. Patient continues to be good candidate for clinical trial enrollment.

At age 64, the patient was evaluated for and enrolled in a phase II clinical trial investigating a KRASG12C-independent SOS1 inhibitor in combination with a next-generation EGFR/MET bispecific antibody (amivantamab-like construct) for EGFR-mutant NSCLC with acquired T790M resistance post-third-generation TKI. The patient meets eligibility criteria: EGFR L858R primary mutation, acquired T790M, post-osimertinib progression, ECOG PS 0-1, adequate organ function, no prior bispecific antibody therapy. Pemetrexed maintenance discontinued. Trial-required additional tumor biopsy performed (right lower lobe CT-guided, fresh specimen sent to central laboratory). Enrollment signed and confirmed. ECOG PS 1. Cancer present, stable on prior maintenance therapy.

At age 64 years, the patient was enrolled and initiated on a phase II investigational clinical trial combining an EGFR/MET bispecific monoclonal antibody (investigational agent JBI-602-like construct) with an oral SOS1 inhibitor (BI 1701963 analog) for EGFR-mutant NSCLC with T790M acquired resistance. Trial regimen: Investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody administered intravenously every 2 weeks plus oral SOS1 inhibitor daily on a 28-day cycle. This replaces pemetrexed maintenance. Treatment intent: palliative/disease-control. The patient received cycle 1 day 1 without infusion-related reactions. Pre-medications: antihistamine, acetaminophen, and corticosteroid administered prior to IV infusion.

At age 64 (approximately 3 weeks into clinical trial treatment), the patient developed grade 2 infusion-related reaction during the cycle 1 day 15 bispecific antibody infusion, manifesting as flushing, urticaria, and mild hypotension (BP 88/52 mmHg transiently). Infusion was paused. Diphenhydramine 25 mg IV and hydrocortisone 100 mg IV administered. Blood pressure normalized within 20 minutes. Infusion resumed at 50% rate without further reaction. Additionally, grade 1-2 acneiform rash (EGFR-related) re-emerged on the face and neck. Managed with topical clindamycin. Grade 1 nausea from oral SOS1 inhibitor managed with ondansetron PRN. No dose modifications required. Pre-medications enhanced for all subsequent infusions.

At age 64 (after 2 cycles of clinical trial therapy, approximately 8 weeks on study), the patient underwent protocol-mandated restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain. CT findings: Right lower lobe mass decreased from 2.3 cm to 1.7 cm (26% reduction, partial response). Right adrenal metastasis decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm. Bilateral pulmonary nodules no longer visualized (resolved). No new sites of disease. MRI brain: Previously treated cerebellar and frontal lesions show continued radiation necrosis with further volume reduction. No new intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal disease. Impression: Partial response to clinical trial therapy. Encouraging early response. Cancer present, responding to investigational therapy.

At age 64, the patient completed 4 cycles of clinical trial therapy (EGFR/MET bispecific antibody plus SOS1 inhibitor). She is tolerating therapy well with management of EGFR-related rash (grade 1, well-controlled on topical regimen) and improved infusion management protocol. ECOG PS 1. Restaging imaging after 2 cycles demonstrated partial response with right lower lobe primary reduced to 1.7 cm and adrenal metastasis reduced to 0.7 cm, with resolution of bilateral pulmonary nodules. The patient reports improved energy, resolution of dyspnea, and the ability to continue part-time teaching. She remains neurologically intact with no new CNS symptoms. Peripheral neuropathy from prior carboplatin remains grade 1 (stable). Overall assessment: Continued clinical benefit on trial therapy. Cancer present, ongoing partial response. Next restaging imaging at 4-month mark per protocol. Continue current trial regimen.

documents.text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION:
62-year-old female with a 6-week history of progressive exertional dyspnea, right-sided chest discomfort, and unintentional weight loss of approximately 4 kg. Former smoker (12 pack-years, quit approximately 18 years ago). Referred by primary care for evaluation of possible pulmonary pathology. History of hypertension and hypothyroidism.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis performed with intravenous contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL). Axial images obtained with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformats at 1.25 mm slice thickness through the chest and 2.5 mm through the abdomen and pelvis. Lung windows, mediastinal windows, and bone windows reviewed. No prior imaging available for comparison.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs/Airways: There is a 4.8 cm spiculated, irregular mass centered in the right lower lobe (series 4, image 112), with surrounding mild ground-glass opacity at the margins. No satellite nodules are identified in the right lower lobe. The central airways are patent and unremarkable. The left lung demonstrates no focal consolidation, mass, or nodule. No air space disease bilaterally. Mild bilateral dependent atelectasis noted at the lung bases.

Mediastinum/Hila: Ipsilateral mediastinal adenopathy is present. Multiple enlarged right paratracheal lymph nodes at station 4R are noted, the largest measuring 1.6 cm in short-axis diameter. Subcarinal lymph node mass (station 7) measures 2.1 cm in short-axis diameter, with loss of normal fatty hilum and rounded morphology. No contralateral (left-sided) mediastinal adenopathy identified. No significant hilar adenopathy on the left. Right hilar region shows borderline fullness; discrete enlarged nodes difficult to separate from vascular structures on this non-FDG study.

Pleura/Pericardium: No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion. No pleural thickening or nodularity to suggest pleural involvement.

Heart/Great Vessels: The cardiac silhouette is mildly enlarged but within limits for technique. Mild coronary artery calcification in the left anterior descending distribution. No pericardial effusion. The aorta demonstrates mild ectasia without aneurysm. Main pulmonary artery is normal in caliber. No filling defect to suggest central pulmonary embolism on arterial phase images.

Chest Wall/Osseous Structures: No rib erosion or destruction. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesion in the visualized spine or ribs. Mild multilevel thoracic degenerative changes. No chest wall invasion by the right lower lobe mass.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with homogeneous parenchymal attenuation on portal venous phase imaging. No focal hepatic lesion. No biliary ductal dilation. Portal vein patent.

Gallbladder/Biliary: Gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct normal in caliber.

Pancreas: The pancreas enhances homogeneously. No focal mass, ductal dilation, or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and morphology. No nodule or mass identified bilaterally. The right adrenal gland demonstrates the expected inverted Y configuration without focal thickening. No adrenal adenoma or metastatic deposit identified.

Kidneys/Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically. No hydronephrosis. A simple cortical cyst measures 1.2 cm in the left kidney lower pole, too small to fully characterize but without worrisome features. No solid renal lesion.

Gastrointestinal: Oral contrast was not administered. Stomach and loops of bowel are unremarkable without wall thickening, obstruction, or free air. No mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Retroperitoneum/Vasculature: The abdominal aorta and iliac vessels are patent without aneurysmal dilation. No retroperitoneal or para-aortic lymphadenopathy.

PELVIS:

Urinary Bladder: Moderately distended. No wall thickening or intraluminal lesion.

Uterus/Adnexa: The uterus is small and atrophic, consistent with postmenopausal status. No adnexal mass. No pelvic free fluid.

Osseous Pelvis: No lytic or blastic lesion. Mild bilateral hip degenerative changes.

IMPRESSION:

1. Large spiculated 4.8 cm right lower lobe mass with irregular, infiltrative margins and subtle surrounding ground-glass change -- highly worrisome for primary lung malignancy. The morphology and location are most consistent with a primary lung carcinoma; given the patient's smoking history and demographic, adenocarcinoma is a leading consideration, though other histologies cannot be excluded without tissue.

2. Ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy with enlarged, morphologically abnormal right paratracheal (station 4R, up to 1.6 cm short axis) and subcarinal (station 7, 2.1 cm short axis) lymph nodes. Involvement of ipsilateral mediastinal nodal stations raises concern for N2 disease.

3. No evidence of distant metastatic involvement of the liver, adrenal glands, kidneys, or intraabdominal/pelvic organs on this study.

4. No pleural effusion or pleural disease.

5. No osseous lesion suspicious for metastatic disease in the visualized skeleton.

Correlation with PET/CT is recommended to better characterize the metabolic activity of the right lower lobe mass and mediastinal nodes and to assess for occult distant disease. Tissue biopsy will be necessary for definitive histologic diagnosis. Given the absence of brain imaging, dedicated MRI brain with contrast is suggested as part of complete staging evaluation.

CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST — RESTAGING EXAMINATION

INDICATION: 62-year-old female with stage IIIB right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant. Currently on osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily for approximately 3 months. Restaging evaluation for treatment response assessment.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained at initial staging (approximately 3 months prior).

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous iohexol contrast administered in the portal venous phase. Axial images acquired with coronal and sagittal reconstructions. Slice thickness 1.5 mm.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The previously described right lower lobe spiculated mass has undergone appreciable interval reduction in size, now measuring approximately 2.9 cm in greatest axial dimension compared to 4.8 cm on the prior examination. The mass retains its spiculated, irregular margins. There is no significant cavitation. The adjacent pleura remains without evidence of invasion or thickening. No endobronchial lesion identified. No new pulmonary nodules. The visualized bronchi are patent. The left lung is clear without focal consolidation, mass, or nodule.

Mediastinum and Hilum: The previously noted right paratracheal (station 4R) and subcarinal (station 7) lymphadenopathy has markedly decreased. The right paratracheal lymph node cluster previously measuring up to approximately 1.6 cm in short axis now measures approximately 7 mm, below the threshold for pathologic enlargement by size criteria. The subcarinal nodal conglomerate, previously prominent and confluent, has dramatically decreased and is now represented by a small residual soft tissue density measuring approximately 8 mm in short axis. The right and left hila are unremarkable. No additional mediastinal adenopathy is identified.

Heart and Pericardium: Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion. The great vessels are patent. Aorta and pulmonary vasculature are unremarkable.

Pleura: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Soft Tissues and Bones: Mild degenerative changes of the thoracic spine. No suspicious lytic or sclerotic osseous lesion. Axillary lymph nodes are not enlarged. Soft tissue structures of the chest wall are unremarkable.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal lesion identified. The biliary system is not dilated. No perihepatic fluid.

Gallbladder: Unremarkable. No gallstones or wall thickening.

Pancreas: Normal size and morphology. No ductal dilation or peripancreatic stranding.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal abnormality.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass identified bilaterally. The right adrenal gland, though partially limited by technique on this phase, demonstrates no discrete nodularity compared to the prior examination.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys are normal in size with preserved cortical thickness. Small bilateral cortical cysts, measuring up to 8 mm, are simple in appearance and unchanged. No hydronephrosis. No perirenal abnormality.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel are within normal limits. No bowel obstruction. No free air. No ascites.

Retroperitoneum and Mesentery: No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No mesenteric mass or adenopathy.

Abdominal Vasculature: The aorta is normal in caliber. No significant atherosclerotic disease.

---

PELVIS:

Uterus and Ovaries: The uterus is normal in size. No adnexal mass. No pelvic free fluid.

Bladder: Normal in appearance and adequately distended.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes.

Osseous Pelvis: No aggressive osseous lesion. Mild degenerative changes at the bilateral sacroiliac joints.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Right lower lobe primary mass has decreased substantially in size, now measuring approximately 2.9 cm compared to 4.8 cm at initial staging — a marked interval reduction consistent with a meaningful treatment response to targeted therapy.

2. Mediastinal lymphadenopathy has improved dramatically. Previously enlarged right paratracheal (4R) and subcarinal (7) nodal stations are now sub-centimeter, with no residual bulky adenopathy.

3. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion.

4. No hepatic, adrenal, or intraabdominal metastatic disease.

5. Overall, the imaging findings reflect a favorable response to osimertinib. The right lower lobe primary and mediastinal nodal disease have responded substantially since the prior study.

THORACIC ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Margaret Tran | DOB on file | MRN on file
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]
Visit type: In-person follow-up

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Three-month follow-up for stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma on osimertinib (Tagrisso). Restaging imaging reviewed today.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Tran is a 62-year-old Vietnamese-American woman and former smoker (12 pack-years, quit ~18 years ago) with a history of well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism who was diagnosed approximately 3 months ago with stage IIIB (T3N2M0) lung adenocarcinoma of the right lower lobe. Her initial presentation was progressive exertional dyspnea over 6 weeks, right-sided chest discomfort, and roughly 4 kg of unintentional weight loss. CT chest demonstrated a 4.8 cm spiculated RLL mass with ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal adenopathy (stations 4R, 7). PET confirmed FDG-avid disease at these sites without distant metastases (SUVmax 14.2 primary; 9.8 and 11.3 at 4R and subcarinal nodes, respectively). Brain MRI was negative for intracranial disease at staging.

CT-guided transthoracic biopsy of the RLL mass revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns, TTF-1 positive, Napsin A positive, PD-L1 TPS 15% by 22C3. Comprehensive tissue NGS (523-gene panel) identified an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation (AF 34.2%) with co-mutant TP53 p.R175H. ALK, ROS1, MET, KRAS, ERBB2, and RET alterations were all negative/absent. TMB low at 6.2 mutations/Mb, MSS.

Given the EGFR-driver mutation and patient preference to avoid upfront chemoradiation toxicity, the multidisciplinary tumor board elected to proceed with osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily as first-line therapy, with discussion of the LAURA trial data. She initiated osimertinib approximately 3 months ago.

Since starting Tagrisso, Ms. Tran has noted the following:
- Significant improvement in exertional dyspnea. She notes she can now walk her usual distance without stopping and is no longer breathless during routine classroom activities.
- Right chest discomfort has resolved.
- Weight: she has regained approximately 2 kg. Currently back to near her prior weight.
- Skin: she developed a grade 2 acneiform rash involving the face, neck, and upper chest approximately 6 weeks after starting Tagrisso. This was managed with oral doxycycline 100 mg BID and topical clindamycin 1% gel. She reports the rash has substantially improved and she would now describe it as mild -- scattered papules on the forehead and chin, not painful, not cosmetically distressing. She has been compliant with the topical regimen and is pleased with the response to doxycycline.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Tran is a 63-year-old Vietnamese-American woman with stage IV (M1c) lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 21 L858R-mutant, TP53 co-mutant, who presents today for oncology follow-up following completion of 4 cycles of second-line platinum-based chemotherapy. Her disease history is notable for initial presentation approximately 16 months ago with a 4.8 cm right lower lobe mass and ipsilateral mediastinal adenopathy (stations 4R and 7), staged at IIIB (T3N2M0). She was treated with first-line osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily with an excellent initial response — primary mass shrunk to 1.8 cm by 9 months and mediastinal nodes cleared — but developed radiographic and pathologic progression at 14 months. At that time, restaging demonstrated re-enlargement of the right lower lobe primary to 3.1 cm, a new right adrenal metastasis (1.4 cm), new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and recurrent station 4R adenopathy. Brain MRI simultaneously revealed three new intracranial metastases (right cerebellum 1.1 cm, left frontal 0.8 cm, right parietal 0.6 cm). Repeat tissue biopsy of the adrenal metastasis confirmed metastatic lung adenocarcinoma, and NGS from that specimen identified acquired EGFR T790M as the dominant resistance mechanism, with subclonal PIK3CA E545K and low-level CDK4 amplification as potential co-contributors. No high-level MET amplification, no ERBB2 amplification, no bypass track fusions identified.

She subsequently underwent Gamma Knife SRS to all three brain metastases (right cerebellum 20 Gy/1 fx, left frontal 22 Gy/1 fx, right parietal 24 Gy/1 fx), tolerated without acute neurological sequelae. Post-SRS she experienced grade 2 fatigue and a brief grade 1 headache managed with acetaminophen and a dexamethasone taper. Osimertinib was discontinued at the time of confirmed progression, and carboplatin AUC 5 plus pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 every 21 days was initiated as second-line systemic therapy. Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 3 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 42K on day 14) and grade 2 anemia (Hgb 8.9 g/dL) requiring carboplatin dose reduction to AUC 4 and a one-week delay for cycle 3; she received a single-donor platelet transfusion for symptomatic epistaxis. No febrile neutropenia. Restaging CT after cycle 3 demonstrated reduction of the right lower lobe mass from 3.1 cm to 2.4 cm, stable adrenal metastasis, stable bilateral pulmonary nodules, and an excellent intracranial response to SRS (cerebellar lesion 0.6 cm with central necrosis, frontal and parietal lesions continuing to regress). She has now completed cycle 4 (carboplatin AUC 4/pemetrexed full dose), and today's visit is focused on assessment for maintenance therapy and clinical trial eligibility.

She reports that fatigue remains her most bothersome complaint — she describes it as a "heavy tiredness" that limits her afternoon activities, roughly grade 1-2. She managed to teach part-time through most of the chemotherapy cycles, though took several sick days around days 10-14 of each cycle when fatigue and nausea peaked. Dyspnea has improved substantially compared to her baseline at progression; she is now walking 1-2 miles without significant breathlessness. Nausea has been manageable with scheduled ondansetron 8 mg, which she continues to use on treatment days and PRN. She notes some residual fingertip paresthesias bilaterally, right more than left, which she attributes to the carboplatin — no burning dysesthesias, no functional impairment. CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS:
1. The right lower lobe primary lung mass has decreased in size from 3.1 cm to approximately 2.4 cm, in keeping with a meaningful response to carbo/Alimta chemotherapy.
2. The previously confirmed right adrenal metastasis is unchanged at 1.4 cm - stable over the current treatment period.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known EGFR L858R-mutant right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, stage IIIB at diagnosis, initially treated with osimertinib. Recent restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrated enlargement of the right lower lobe primary mass, a new right adrenal nodule, new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and recurrent mediastinal adenopathy, raising concern for systemic progression after approximately 14 months of targeted therapy. MRI brain obtained urgently to evaluate for intracranial involvement.

TECHNIQUE: MRI of the brain was performed on a 3.0 Tesla magnet. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1 MPRAGE pre-contrast, axial T2 FLAIR, axial diffusion-weighted imaging with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial gradient echo (GRE/SWI), axial T1 pre-contrast, and axial and coronal T1 post-contrast following intravenous administration of gadobutrol 0.1 mmol/kg. Multiplanar reformatted images reviewed.

COMPARISON: MRI brain with and without contrast performed at the time of initial staging evaluation (approximately 14 months prior): no intracranial metastases.

---

FINDINGS:

INTRACRANIAL METASTATIC LESIONS:
Three discrete enhancing parenchymal lesions are identified, all new since the prior brain MRI:

1. RIGHT CEREBELLAR HEMISPHERE: A well-circumscribed, avidly ring-enhancing lesion measuring approximately 1.1 cm in greatest axial dimension is identified in the right cerebellar hemisphere, lateral to the midline. The lesion demonstrates a hypointense core on T1 pre-contrast with thin, irregular peripheral enhancement post-gadolinium. On T2/FLAIR, there is a modest halo of surrounding hyperintense signal consistent with vasogenic edema extending into the adjacent cerebellar white matter. No evidence of internal hemorrhage on GRE/susceptibility-weighted imaging. No herniation or tonsillar descent. No significant mass effect on the fourth ventricle.

2. LEFT FRONTAL LOBE: A smaller enhancing lesion measuring approximately 0.8 cm is identified at the gray-white junction of the left frontal lobe, in the posterior aspect of the superior frontal gyrus. Post-gadolinium sequences demonstrate solid nodular enhancement. Mild surrounding T2/FLAIR signal change is present. No hemorrhagic component on susceptibility imaging. No cortical involvement or sulcal effacement.

3. RIGHT PARIETAL LOBE: A third lesion measuring approximately 0.6 cm is identified at the gray-white junction of the right parietal lobe, in the region of the postcentral gyrus. Solid nodular enhancement is noted on post-contrast sequences. Minimal associated T2/FLAIR signal abnormality. No hemorrhage. No significant mass effect.

All three lesions are located at or near the gray-white junction, a distribution highly characteristic of hematogenous metastatic spread. The enhancing morphology, clinical context, and pattern of involvement are most consistent with metastatic disease.

LEPTOMENINGEAL DISEASE: No sulcal, cisternal, or ependymal enhancement to suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis. The cranial nerves are unremarkable bilaterally.

VENTRICLES AND CISTERNS: The ventricular system is normal in caliber. There is no obstructive hydrocephalus. Basal cisterns are patent.

BRAIN PARENCHYMA: No territorial infarct. No areas of restricted diffusion to suggest acute ischemic injury. White matter signal changes are mild and nonspecific, age-appropriate. Cortical thickness and gyral pattern are preserved. No evidence of encephalomalacia.

EXTRA-AXIAL SPACES: No subdural or epidural collection. Calvarium and skull base are intact without suspicious osseous lesion. Visualized paranasal sinuses show trace mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus, likely incidental.

PITUITARY AND POSTERIOR FOSSA: Pituitary gland appears normal without a discrete adenoma. No acoustic schwannoma. Craniocervical junction is unremarkable.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Three new enhancing parenchymal lesions compared to prior MRI brain obtained approximately 14 months ago, at the time of initial staging. The lesions are located in the right cerebellar hemisphere (1.1 cm, ring-enhancing with surrounding edema), left frontal lobe (0.8 cm, solid enhancement), and right parietal lobe (0.6 cm, solid enhancement). The pattern, distribution at the gray-white junction, and clinical context are consistent with intracranial metastases from the patient's known lung adenocarcinoma.

2. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

3. No evidence of acute intracranial hemorrhage.

4. No herniation or hydrocephalus. Mass effect from the cerebellar lesion is limited and does not appear to threaten the fourth ventricle or cerebellar tonsils at this time.

Correlation with systemic restaging findings and multidisciplinary neurosurgical and radiation oncology input is recommended for treatment planning.

The histologic and immunophenotypic features of this right adrenal core biopsy are diagnostic of metastatic adenocarcinoma of pulmonary origin. The tumor cells demonstrate strong TTF-1 and Napsin A positivity with CK7 expression, a profile highly specific for lung adenocarcinoma primary in this clinical context. INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
This molecular profile is obtained from a metastatic right adrenal lesion at the time of clinical and radiographic progression following approximately 14 months of first-line osimertinib (TAGRISSO) therapy for EGFR L858R-mutant stage IIIB/IV lung adenocarcinoma.

The dominant finding is the ACQUISITION of an EGFR p.T790M (exon 20) mutation at a variant allele frequency of 19.4%, which was absent in the pre-treatment baseline specimen. This is the most well-characterized mechanism of acquired resistance to third-generation EGFR TKIs, including osimertinib, and occurs in approximately 10–15% of patients progressing on this agent. Mechanistically, T790M in the context of prior osimertinib treatment reflects re-emergence of a resistant subclone — osimertinib's irreversible binding to the mutant EGFR kinase is circumvented through a conformational change at the gatekeeper residue, partially restoring wild-type-like ATP affinity. The retained EGFR L858R driver mutation (28.1% VAF) confirms ongoing EGFR pathway dependence of the dominant clone.

Critically, EGFR C797S — a tertiary mutation that would abolish covalent binding of all currently approved EGFR TKIs and most investigational agents — is NOT detected in either tissue or plasma. This is an important negative finding that preserves potential therapeutic options targeting EGFR in the cis or trans configuration.

Also of note is the subclonal, newly acquired PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (8.3% VAF), detected in tissue but below plasma sensitivity threshold. Subclonal PI3K pathway activation has been described as a secondary resistance mechanism following EGFR TKI exposure, potentially permitting EGFR-independent proliferative signaling through AKT/mTOR. At its present low clonal fraction, this is not currently a dominant resistance driver but warrants monitoring with serial ctDNA.

The newly acquired CDK4 amplification (CN 6.2), in the setting of pre-existing CDKN2A heterozygous deletion (retained from baseline), suggests convergent deregulation of the G1/S checkpoint. CDK4 amplification has been reported as an acquired resistance mechanism across multiple oncogene-driven NSCLC contexts. Whether CDK4/6 inhibition would be clinically useful in this setting remains investigational.

High-level MET amplification — one of the most clinically important EGFR-bypass resistance mechanisms (present in ~5–10% of osimertinib progressors) — is NOT present. MET gain is borderline (CN 3.8) and below the accepted amplification threshold, consistent across tissue and plasma. This is relevant because MET bypass typically predicts sensitivity to combined EGFR/MET inhibition strategies.

No fusion-based bypass mechanisms (RET, NTRK, FGFR, NRG1, etc.) were identified.

TMB remains low (5.8 mut/Mb) and MSS status is confirmed, consistent with the baseline profile and the known biology of EGFR-driven lung adenocarcinoma. Immunotherapy monotherapy is unlikely to provide meaningful benefit in this molecular context.

In summary, the predominant acquired resistance mechanism to osimertinib in this patient is EGFR T790M, with secondary subclonal alterations in PIK3CA and CDK4 potentially contributing. The absence of high-level MET amplification, ERBB2 amplification, C797S, and fusion-based bypass mechanisms are all clinically meaningful negative findings.

ACTIONABILITY SUMMARY
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  TIER I (Standard of Care / Guideline-Supported):
    - EGFR T790M (acquired): Historically targeted by osimertinib, but T790M here
      represents on-target resistance TO osimertinib rather than T790M in a first-
      generation TKI-resistant patient. Standard second-line options for post-
      osimertinib T790M are not well-defined; platinum-doublet chemotherapy represents
      the current guideline-concordant approach for systemic progression.

  TIER II (Investigational / Clinical Trial Priority):
    - EGFR L858R + T790M (post-osimertinib, C797S-negative): Potentially targetable by
      bispecific EGFR/MET antibody combinations (e.g., amivantamab-based regimens),
      fourth-generation EGFR TKI approaches (e.g., BLU-701, BBT-176), or allosteric
      EGFR inhibitors. Clinical trial enrollment strongly encouraged.
    - PIK3CA E545K (subclonal): PI3K/AKT pathway co-targeting (alpelisib, capivasertib)
      in combination with EGFR-directed agents — investigational.
    - CDK4 amplification: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) in
      EGFR-mutant NSCLC — investigational; no established standard indication.

  TIER IV (Not Actionable at This Time):
    - TP53 p.R175H: No approved targeted therapy; prognostic co-mutation only.
    - MET low-level gain (CN 3.8): Below amplification threshold; not actionable.
    - ERBB2 low-level gain (CN 3.1): Below HER2 amplification threshold; no
      activating mutation; not actionable.
    - MSS, low TMB: Immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy not supported.

ASSESSMENT
Ms. Tran is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV (now M1b/M1c — adrenal and brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma with documented radiographic and pathological progression after 14 months of first-line osimertinib. The dominant acquired resistance mechanism is EGFR T790M (confirmed on adrenal metastasis NGS and concordant with plasma ctDNA), without evidence of an actionable bypass track (no high-level MET amplification, no ERBB2 amplification, no new fusion event). EGFR C797S — which would be the expected further resistance mutation had she been rechallenged with a third-gen TKI — is absent, consistent with early evolution. Subclonal PIK3CA E545K and CDK4 amplification are of uncertain clinical significance at this time.

The brain metastases are asymptomatic but require treatment; given only three discrete lesions, favorable performance status (ECOG 1), and the absence of leptomeningeal disease, stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) is strongly preferred over whole-brain radiation therapy (WBRT). Systemic progression is confirmed and requires a change in systemic therapy. Platinum-doublet chemotherapy (carboplatin + pemetrexed) is the appropriate next systemic line for this EGFR-mutant patient after third-generation TKI failure, with a reasonable expected response in adenocarcinoma histology. Clinical trial options for post-osimertinib T790M-bearing NSCLC were discussed with the patient and family at length; no immediately available suitable trial is open at this institution at present, but referrals will be made to regional centers.

This case was reviewed today at multidisciplinary tumor board with input from radiation oncology, neurosurgery, and thoracic medical oncology. All parties are in agreement with the management plan below.

She achieved an initial partial response (primary mass reduced from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm, mediastinal adenopathy cleared) lasting approximately 14 months before developing confirmed radiographic and pathologic progression. At that time, restaging identified growth in the primary RLL mass (3.1 cm), a new right adrenal metastasis, new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and three new brain metastases (right cerebellar 1.1 cm, left frontal 0.8 cm, right parietal 0.6 cm). Resistance biopsy of the adrenal lesion confirmed acquired EGFR T790M mutation as the dominant resistance mechanism (AF 19.4%), with subclonal PIK3CA E545K and low-level CDK4 amplification also noted. Osimertinib was discontinued.

She subsequently underwent Gamma Knife SRS to all three intracranial lesions, followed by second-line carboplatin (AUC 5, later dose-reduced to AUC 4 for grade 3 thrombocytopenia) plus pemetrexed (Alimta) x4 cycles, achieving a partial response in the chest and excellent intracranial control on MRI. She then transitioned to single-agent pemetrexed maintenance while completing clinical trial screening.

She enrolled on the current Phase II trial and received her first infusion of the EGFR/MET bispecific antibody (JBI-602-like construct) plus the oral SOS1 inhibitor approximately 16 weeks ago. Protocol-mandated restaging CT/MRI after cycle 2 showed encouraging partial response — the RLL primary decreased from 2.3 cm to 1.7 cm, adrenal metastasis decreased from 1.1 cm to 0.7 cm, and bilateral pulmonary nodules resolved entirely. Intracranially, the previously treated lesions continued to show radiation necrosis/volume reduction with no new CNS disease.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
---------------------------------------------------------------------
Histologic sections demonstrate a moderately differentiated invasive pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The tumor is characterized by a mixed growth pattern with acinar architecture predominating (approximately 60%) and a secondary lepidic component (approximately 30%). A minor solid component is present (<10%) without evidence of mucin production. The acinar glands are lined by cuboidal to columnar neoplastic epithelial cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and moderate to abundant pale eosinophilic cytoplasm. Mitotic figures are present at approximately 4-5 per 10 high-power fields. No atypical mitoses identified. The lepidic areas demonstrate neoplastic cells spreading along intact alveolar walls without destruction of alveolar architecture. No extensive central necrosis identified; focal comedonecrosis within acinar lumens present in one core fragment.

The background lung parenchyma adjacent to the tumor shows mild chronic inflammation and a mild degree of peribronchiolar fibrosis. No granulomatous inflammation identified. No neuroendocrine features (trabecular/nested pattern, salt-and-pepper chromatin, or Azzopardi effect) are present. Vascular invasion: present, focal, in one of three cores. Perineural invasion: not identified.

Marcus staining of an additional slide confirms presence of mucin within approximately 5% of glandular lumens (mucicarmine, focal weak positivity).

The morphologic and immunophenotypic features are diagnostic of a primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma. The mixed acinar-predominant and lepidic growth pattern, together with strong TTF-1 and Napsin A positivity, are characteristic of lung adenocarcinoma. Squamous cell carcinoma and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma are excluded by negative p40, p63, synaptophysin, chromogranin, and CD56. The absence of CK20 expression argues against a primary colorectal or urothelial origin. The relatively low PD-L1 TPS of 15% is noted and falls in the low-expression category; however, definitive therapeutic implications should be considered in conjunction with molecular profiling results.

METHODOLOGY
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DNA was extracted from macrodissected FFPE tumor tissue and matched peripheral blood. Library preparation was performed using a hybridization capture-based approach targeting 523 cancer-relevant genes, including full coding exons, hotspot regions, and selected intronic regions for fusion partner detection. Paired-end sequencing was performed on the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform. Variant calling was performed using a validated bioinformatics pipeline with filters for strand bias, base quality, and mapping quality. Somatic variants were confirmed by subtraction of matched germline variants. Copy number analysis was performed using a log2 ratio method normalized to matched germline. Fusion detection utilized split-read and discordant read-pair analysis. Microsatellite instability (MSI) was assessed by interrogating 114 microsatellite loci. Tumor mutational burden (TMB) was calculated as the number of somatic nonsynonymous mutations per megabase of sequenced coding territory. Germline analysis covered 84 hereditary cancer predisposition genes per American College of Medical Genetics (ACMG) guidelines.

Analytical sensitivity: >98% for substitutions and small indels with allele frequency ≥5% at adequate depth; >95% for copy number alterations with ≥4-fold amplification.

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
INTERPRETATION
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
The most clinically significant finding in this tumor specimen is an EGFR p.L858R missense mutation in exon 21 (VAF 34.2%). This is one of the two most common activating mutations in EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer (the other being exon 19 deletions), representing approximately 40-45% of all EGFR-mutant NSCLC cases. The L858R substitution results in constitutive activation of the EGFR kinase domain through stabilization of the active conformation, driving downstream oncogenic signaling through the RAS/MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and STAT pathways.

Importantly, this specimen was obtained prior to any systemic therapy exposure. The absence of EGFR T790M at baseline is consistent with a de novo treatment-naive tumor and does not preclude the development of T790M as an acquired resistance mechanism following EGFR TKI therapy.

The co-occurring TP53 p.R175H mutation (VAF 41.8%) is a well-characterized hotspot gain-of-function mutation in the DNA-binding domain of p53. TP53 co-mutations are identified in approximately 40-60% of EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinomas. Published data suggest that TP53 co-mutations — particularly exon 5 hotspot mutations including R175H — may be associated with somewhat shorter progression-free survival on first- and second-generation EGFR TKIs, though the prognostic impact with third-generation inhibitors such as osimertinib remains an area of active investigation.

Heterozygous deletion of CDKN2A (encoding p16/INK4A and p14/ARF) is a common secondary alteration in lung adenocarcinoma and may contribute to cell cycle dysregulation but is not currently independently actionable in this context.

The following negative findings are particularly noteworthy:
- No KRAS mutation: KRAS and EGFR mutations are biologically mutually exclusive in lung adenocarcinoma and the absence of KRAS is concordant with the EGFR-mutant genotype.
- ALK, ROS1, RET fusions: Negative, consistent with EGFR-mutant biology (co-occurrence of EGFR with these fusions is exceedingly rare).
- STK11 and KEAP1: Wild type. The absence of STK11/KEAP1 co-mutations is favorable, as these alterations are associated with resistance to immunotherapy and potentially with altered biology in EGFR-mutant tumors.
- MET exon 14 skipping and MET amplification: Not detected at baseline, relevant for future resistance assessment.
- TMB low (6.2 mut/Mb) and MSS: Consistent with typical EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma biology. This tumor is unlikely to benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition as primary therapy, consistent with published data (e.g., IMpower150 subset analyses and CheckMate trials demonstrating limited benefit of immunotherapy monotherapy in EGFR-mutant NSCLC).

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Tran is a 62-year-old Vietnamese-American woman who presents to the thoracic oncology clinic for initial consultation following recent diagnosis of right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, stage IIIB (T3N2M0), with EGFR L858R mutation confirmed on tissue NGS.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Tran is a 62-year-old woman with a history of well-controlled hypertension, hypothyroidism, and a remote 12 pack-year smoking history (quit approximately 18 years ago) who was referred to her PCP approximately 6-8 weeks ago with progressive exertional dyspnea, mild right-sided chest discomfort, and approximately 4 kg unintentional weight loss over that same period. She initially attributed her symptoms to seasonal allergies and occupational stress as a high school biology teacher, but the persistence and progression of her dyspnea prompted evaluation.

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed at that time demonstrated a 4.8 cm spiculated right lower lobe mass with ipsilateral mediastinal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy (stations 4R and 7). No pleural effusion. CT abdomen/pelvis showed no hepatic, adrenal, or intraabdominal abnormalities at that time. PET/CT subsequently confirmed an FDG-avid right lower lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2) and hypermetabolic mediastinal nodes (station 4R SUVmax 9.8, subcarinal SUVmax 11.3) with no distant FDG-avid lesions. MRI brain with and without contrast showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

CT-guided transthoracic needle biopsy of the right lower lobe mass confirmed moderately differentiated lung adenocarcinoma with acinar and lepidic growth patterns. IHC: TTF-1 positive, Napsin A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative. PD-L1 TPS by 22C3 assay: 15%. Tissue NGS (523-gene panel, tumor-normal paired analysis) returned and is notable for: EGFR p.L858R exon 21 activating mutation (allele frequency 34.2%, tier 1 actionable); TP53 p.R175H co-mutation (allele frequency 41.8%); STK11 wild type; KEAP1 wild type; ALK/ROS1/RET fusions: all negative; KRAS: wild type; MET copy number 2.1 (no amplification); TMB: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low); MSS. No germline pathogenic variants identified.

The case was reviewed at our multidisciplinary thoracic oncology tumor board today with representation from thoracic surgery, radiation oncology, and medical oncology.

Thoracic Surgery Assessment: Given the presence of bulky ipsilateral mediastinal adenopathy at stations 4R and 7, Ms. Tran is not a surgical candidate at this time. Upfront surgical resection is not recommended for stage IIIB N2 disease.

Radiation Oncology Assessment: Concurrent chemoradiation (chemo-RT) per PACIFIC trial paradigm with subsequent Imfinzi consolidation was discussed. However, the team noted that the patient's EGFR mutation status significantly alters this calculus — Imfinzi consolidation post-chemo-RT is of uncertain benefit (and may be inferior) in EGFR-mutant NSCLC based on retrospective subset analyses of PACIFIC. The emerging LAURA trial data supporting osi maintenance after definitive CRT was reviewed in detail.

Medical Oncology Assessment: Given the actionable EGFR L858R mutation, osi (Tagrisso) is the preferred systemic therapy approach. In the context of stage IIIB unresectable disease with EGFR mutation, the following options were discussed with the patient:
  (a) Definitive concurrent chemoradiation followed by osi maintenance (LAURA trial schema)
  (b) Upfront osi as first-line systemic therapy, deferring radiation

The patient was counseled extensively regarding both options, including expected response rates, toxicity profiles, and the anticipated development of acquired resistance. She expressed a strong preference to avoid the acute and cumulative toxicities of thoracic radiation and platinum-based chemotherapy given her desire to continue teaching. After thorough informed discussion, the decision was made to initiate Tagrisso 80 mg orally daily as first-line targeted therapy. The patient understands that this is an individualized decision and that monitoring for resistance is essential.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Tran is a 62-year-old former smoker with newly diagnosed stage IIIB (T3N2M0) right lower lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation, TTF-1/Napsin A positive, PD-L1 TPS 15%, TMB-low (6.2 mut/Mb), co-mutant TP53 R175H. She is not a surgical candidate given N2 mediastinal involvement. After multidisciplinary tumor board review and extensive patient counseling, she and her family have elected to proceed with upfront osi (Tagrisso) targeted therapy rather than concurrent chemoradiation.

1. Initiate Tagrisso (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line systemic therapy for stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant NSCLC.
   - Rationale: Third-generation EGFR TKI; superior CNS penetration; FLAURA data support osi over first/second-generation TKIs; preferred for EGFR-mutant NSCLC regardless of exon 19 del vs L858R subtype.
   - Counseled regarding expected side effects: acneiform/papulopustular rash, paronychia, diarrhea, dry skin, possible QTc prolongation, rare ILD (~3%); patient instructed to contact clinic promptly for new respiratory symptoms.
   - ECOG PS 1 at initiation.

2. Baseline and monitoring:
   - EKG at baseline (QTc 432 ms — acceptable, will recheck at 4 weeks).
   - Thyroid function monitoring given risk of osi-associated thyroid changes and baseline hypothyroidism.
   - CBC, CMP, LFTs at baseline and with each follow-up visit.
   - Ophthalmology referral deferred unless visual symptoms develop.

3. Radiation oncology: Thoracic radiation deferred at this time per patient preference and multidisciplinary discussion. Radiation oncology will remain involved for potential future need or if systemic control proves insufficient.

4. Restaging imaging planned at approximately 3 months (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; MRI brain if clinically indicated).

5. Patient education and support:
   - Provided written materials regarding lung cancer and Tagrisso therapy in English.
   - Interpreter services offered, declined.
   - Referred to lung cancer nurse navigator for ongoing support, symptom management education, and coordination.
   - Advised on skin care regimen prophylactically (gentle cleanser, fragrance-free moisturizer, sunscreen) ahead of anticipated EGFR TKI-related dermatologic side effects.
   - Smoking cessation re-counseling documented (patient already a non-smoker x 18 years).

6. Cancer present. No prior systemic therapy. Plan is to reassess response and tolerance at first follow-up in 4-6 weeks with laboratory evaluation, then formal restaging CT at 3 months.
